constraints.expression: consumer verifies Ed25519 signature over RFC 8785 canonical JSON of all-other-fields using signing_key_id and signed_by
constraints.message: Ed25519 signature over RFC 8785 canonical JSON of all-other-fields MUST verify against signing_key_id and signed_by.
constraints.evaluation_note: Cryptographic signature verification is consumer-side per NF-1 (library/runtime boundary). The library evaluator SKIPS this rule and surfaces it in the UnverifiedObligationsManifest. Consumers MUST: (1) reconstruct RFC 8785 canonical JSON of all fields except `signature`; (2) verify the Ed25519 signature against the public key referenced by `signed_by` and `signing_key_id`; (3) reject the record on signature mismatch or stale key identifier. Verification profile published in docs/architecture/org-overseer.md. The expression carries the narrative obligation rather than a parseable DSL expression because the rule is runtime-deferred.

constraints.expression: revocation.revoked is append-only: once true, MUST NOT be set back to false in any subsequent OrgRepresentativeDelegation record sharing this delegation_id
constraints.message: revocation.revoked is append-only: once revoked (envelope present, revoked == true), no later record sharing this delegation_id may be written without the revocation envelope.
constraints.evaluation_note: Temporal append-only invariant unprovable from a single record's state. The library evaluator SKIPS this rule and surfaces it in the UnverifiedObligationsManifest. The schema layer subsumes the per-record half of the invariant (revoked is pinned to literal true, so a record cannot represent revoked == false). The cross-record half — that no later record sharing this delegation_id may drop the revocation envelope — requires the consumer's state-store to reject writes that un-revoke. Documented as a consumer obligation in docs/architecture/org-overseer.md (chain-of-trust section). The expression carries the narrative obligation rather than a parseable DSL expression because the rule is runtime-deferred.

constraints.message: Delegation chain must form a valid DAG keyed by delegation_id, terminate at the genesis sentinel 'genesis:org-public-key', and have chain_depth <= 20.
constraints.evaluation_note: Reuses the is_valid_dag builtin (FR-C1). The genesis sentinel is encoded as the literal string 'genesis:org-public-key' (stable across runners per SDD section 3.6). Library evaluation is gated on the consumer supplying `granted_by_chain_records` in the validation context — a single OrgRepresentativeDelegation record cannot be chain-validated in isolation. Consumers MUST assemble the full chain (the record under validation plus all ancestors back to and including the genesis-rooted record) and SHOULD include a synthetic terminator record with `delegation_id == 'genesis:org-public-key'` so that the is_valid_dag builtin's id-index resolves the genesis-rooted record's granted_by reference (otherwise the dangling-ref check would misclassify the genesis pointer). The single-record's `chain_depth` is the asserted depth; the rule cross-checks that chain_depth <= 20 and that the chain reaches genesis. CONTEXT-ABSENT BEHAVIOR (open-fail): when `granted_by_chain_records` is omitted from the validation context, the constraint-DSL primitives chosen here resolve to vacuous-true (`is_valid_dag` accepts a non-array as vacuously valid; the De Morgan `!chain.every(...)` clause on a non-array also evaluates to true). The schema-level chain_depth bound (Type.Integer minimum 0 maximum 20) and the granted_by union (UUID v4 OR genesis sentinel literal) remain the structural floor, but ORD-3 itself does NOT close-fail in this case. Consumers SHOULD treat the absence of `granted_by_chain_records` as a configuration error in their integration test suite — not as permission to skip enforcement. DSL FORM NOTE: the third clause uses the De Morgan equivalent `!chain.every(r => r.granted_by != 'genesis:org-public-key')` of `chain.some(r => r.granted_by == 'genesis:org-public-key')` because the constraint-DSL grammar v2.0 carries `.every()` but no `.some()` quantifier; cross-runner authors re-implementing in Go / Python / Rust may use `.some()`-style primitives directly.
